stories.headline: EU delays AI compliance deadlines, makes content labelling mandatory but its icons optional
stories.summary: Europe's parliament voted 423 to 57 to push high-risk AI compliance to late 2027 at the earliest, while the Commission released optional labelling icons for AI-generated content. The labelling obligations are mandatory, but the icons themselves are voluntary and don't establish compliance on their own. Voluntary tools with binding deadlines behind them: the grace period is real, but the clock is running.

claims.claim: EU delays AI compliance deadlines, makes content labelling mandatory but its icons optional
claims.summary_span: EU delays AI compliance deadlines, makes content labelling mandatory but its icons optional
claims.source_span: the legislation postpones obligations on high-risk AI systems... While the icons are optional, the labelling obligations themselves are not
claims.note: All bundled facts in the headline are confirmed by the source.

claims.claim: Europe's parliament voted 423 to 57 to push high-risk AI compliance to late 2027 at the earliest
claims.summary_span: Europe's parliament voted 423 to 57 to push high-risk AI compliance to late 2027 at the earliest
claims.source_span: The European Parliament has granted final approval, by 423 votes to 57 with 174 abstentions, to amendments to the EU AI Act... the legislation postpones obligations on high-risk AI systems, which will now apply from 2 December 2027 for stand-alone systems
claims.note: 'Late 2027 at the earliest' is a fair compression of 2 December 2027 for stand-alone systems (with embedded systems delayed further to 2028); vote count matches exactly.

claims.claim: the Commission released optional labelling icons for AI-generated content
claims.summary_span: the Commission released optional labelling icons for AI-generated content
claims.source_span: The European Commission has developed a set of icons that creators, publishers and other deployers of generative AI systems may use to label their AI-generated content... While the icons are optional
claims.note: Source confirms the Commission developed optional icons for labelling AI-generated content.

claims.claim: The labelling obligations are mandatory
claims.summary_span: The labelling obligations are mandatory
claims.source_span: While the icons are optional, the labelling obligations themselves are not
claims.note: Source explicitly states labelling obligations are not optional.

claims.claim: the icons themselves are voluntary and don't establish compliance on their own
claims.summary_span: the icons themselves are voluntary and don't establish compliance on their own
claims.source_span: While the icons are optional, the labelling obligations themselves are not, and the use of these icons does not by itself establish legal compliance.
claims.note: Source directly confirms both that icons are optional and that using them does not by itself establish compliance.

sections.name: big_picture

stories.headline: A classifier intercepts personal data before any language model sees it
stories.summary: The risk is structural: personal data can reach a language model before the system decides whether it should. ComplianceGate proposes a classifier that screens each query in 7ms, routing sensitive data to local endpoints only. On 600 queries, latency fell 39% and costs dropped 33–52%. No code is public; raise this before your next data-residency design review.

claims.claim: A classifier intercepts personal data before any language model sees it
claims.summary_span: A classifier intercepts personal data before any language model sees it
claims.source_span: A trained encoder classifier sits before any decoder inference, evaluating each query for complexity and data sensitivity, then routing it to an appropriately sized dense model in the appropriate geographic location. PII-containing queries route to local endpoints before any LLM computation begins, making data residency violations structurally impossible.
claims.note: The source confirms the classifier acts before any LLM inference and intercepts PII-containing queries before they reach a model.

claims.claim: The risk is structural: personal data can reach a language model before the system decides whether it should.
claims.summary_span: The risk is structural: personal data can reach a language model before the system decides whether it should.
claims.source_span: Personally identifiable information (PII) in user queries can reach model endpoints before the system determines whether that data should leave its jurisdictional boundary.
claims.note: Direct paraphrase of the source's problem statement; accurately captures the structural risk described.

claims.claim: ComplianceGate proposes a classifier that screens each query in 7ms
claims.summary_span: ComplianceGate proposes a classifier that screens each query in 7ms
claims.source_span: The encoder classifier achieves 99.2% accuracy with near-perfect PII recall at 7ms inference overhead
claims.note: The source confirms the classifier has 7ms inference overhead, consistent with 'screens each query in 7ms'.

claims.claim: routing sensitive data to local endpoints only
claims.summary_span: routing sensitive data to local endpoints only
claims.source_span: PII-containing queries route to local endpoints before any LLM computation begins
claims.note: Source confirms PII queries are routed to local endpoints.

claims.claim: On 600 queries, latency fell 39%
claims.summary_span: On 600 queries, latency fell 39%
claims.source_span: Our evaluation on 600 queries demonstrates 39% median latency reduction
claims.note: Exact figures match the source.

claims.claim: costs dropped 33–52%
claims.summary_span: costs dropped 33–52%
claims.source_span: 33-52% cost savings depending on query distribution
claims.note: Exact range matches the source.

claims.claim: No code is public
claims.summary_span: No code is public
claims.note: The source excerpt does not mention code availability; this cannot be confirmed or denied from the excerpt alone.

stories.headline: Anthropic extends its autonomous-agent approach from code to drug discovery
stories.summary: Anthropic launched Claude Science at a pharma and biotech event, elevating it to the same tier as Claude Code. It runs multi-step research tasks autonomously, with tools for genetics and protein biology. Single-source, no independent benchmarks yet. When an autonomous research agent matches a graduate student's output, who in your organisation decides which questions it asks?

claims.claim: Anthropic extends its autonomous-agent approach from code to drug discovery
claims.summary_span: Anthropic extends its autonomous-agent approach from code to drug discovery
claims.source_span: Anthropic announced Claude Science, a major new product intended to support scientific research in the same way that Claude Code supports software engineering... it has access to tools that make it particularly useful for research in computational biology and drug development.
claims.note: The source confirms Anthropic is extending its autonomous-agent approach (Claude Code) to science including drug discovery.

claims.claim: Anthropic launched Claude Science at a pharma and biotech event
claims.summary_span: Anthropic launched Claude Science at a pharma and biotech event
claims.source_span: At an event for pharmaceutical executives, biotech founders, and researchers on Tuesday, Anthropic announced Claude Science
claims.note: The source confirms the launch at a pharma and biotech event.

claims.claim: Claude Science is elevated to the same tier as Claude Code
claims.summary_span: elevating it to the same tier as Claude Code
claims.source_span: Anthropic's decision to elevate Claude Science to the same rank as Claude Code and Claude Cowork indicates that the company is taking AI's scientific applications very seriously
claims.note: The source explicitly states Claude Science is elevated to the same rank as Claude Code.

claims.claim: Claude Science runs multi-step research tasks autonomously
claims.summary_span: It runs multi-step research tasks autonomously
claims.source_span: Claude Science can autonomously carry out meaningful work when given concise, high-level instructions
claims.note: Autonomous multi-step research is a fair paraphrase of autonomously carrying out meaningful work from high-level instructions.

claims.claim: Claude Science has tools for genetics and protein biology
claims.summary_span: with tools for genetics and protein biology
claims.source_span: It can interface with various tools used in genetics, chemistry, and protein biology
claims.note: The source confirms tools for genetics and protein biology; omitting chemistry is not an error.

claims.claim: Single-source, no independent benchmarks yet
claims.summary_span: Single-source, no independent benchmarks yet
claims.source_span: Anthropic's decision to elevate Claude Science to the same rank as Claude Code and Claude Cowork indicates that the company is taking AI's scientific applications very seriously—or at least wants to give the impression that it is.
claims.note: The source provides no independent benchmark data for Claude Science, and the hedged language supports the single-source trust flag.

stories.headline: Forcing AI agents to show evidence before escalating crypto risk alerts
stories.summary: General-purpose language model agents over-escalate on thin evidence. DeXposure-Claw routes decisions through a graph time-series forecasting model and deterministic stress tests before issuing any supervisory ticket, scoring results against an explicit false-intervention rate. Code is public; benchmarks are self-reported. Does your supervisory AI have a comparable evidence gate, or does the model still escalate directly?

claims.claim: Forcing AI agents to show evidence before escalating crypto risk alerts
claims.summary_span: Forcing AI agents to show evidence before escalating crypto risk alerts
claims.source_span: a forecast-grounded agentic supervision system that routes LLM decisions through structured evidence
claims.note: 'Crypto risk alerts' is a fair plain-English paraphrase of DeFi risk supervision, and 'show evidence before escalating' maps to routing decisions through structured evidence gates before emitting supervisory tickets.

claims.claim: General-purpose language model agents over-escalate on thin evidence.
claims.summary_span: General-purpose language model agents over-escalate on thin evidence.
claims.source_span: General-purpose LLM agents fit this setting poorly: they over-read weak evidence and recommend high-stakes interventions
claims.note: 'Over-escalate on thin evidence' is a valid paraphrase of 'over-read weak evidence and recommend high-stakes interventions'.

claims.claim: DeXposure-Claw routes decisions through a graph time-series forecasting model
claims.summary_span: DeXposure-Claw routes decisions through a graph time-series forecasting model
claims.source_span: routes LLM decisions through structured evidence: (1) DeXposure-FM, a graph time-series foundation model, forecasts future exposure networks
claims.note: 'Graph time-series forecasting model' is an accurate paraphrase of 'graph time-series foundation model' that forecasts.

claims.claim: DeXposure-Claw routes decisions through deterministic stress tests before issuing any supervisory ticket
claims.summary_span: deterministic stress tests before issuing any supervisory ticket
claims.source_span: deterministic monitors and stress scenarios then turn those forecasts into typed alerts, attribution signals, and scenario evidence; and (3) data-health and confidence gates constrain escalation before DeXposure-Claw emits auditable supervisory tickets
claims.note: 'Deterministic stress tests' is a fair compression of 'deterministic monitors and stress scenarios', and the sequencing before ticket emission is accurate.

claims.claim: DeXposure-Claw scores results against an explicit false-intervention rate.
claims.summary_span: scoring results against an explicit false-intervention rate
claims.source_span: decision axis scores tickets against a regulator-aligned absolute-loss ground truth and an explicit false-intervention rate
claims.note: The explicit false-intervention rate is directly mentioned in the source as part of the evaluation harness.

claims.claim: Code is public.
claims.summary_span: Code is public
claims.source_span: Code is at this https URL.
claims.note: The source confirms code is publicly available at a URL.

claims.claim: Benchmarks are self-reported.
claims.summary_span: benchmarks are self-reported
claims.source_span: Experiments on five years of weekly real data fully support our system.
claims.note: This is a pre-peer-review arXiv paper with no external validation mentioned; flagging benchmarks as self-reported is an appropriate trust qualifier for this context.

sections.intro_body: Across compliance, research, and risk alerting, the structural choice of where a decision gets made is doing more governance work than any policy document. Design is the control.

sections.name: hands_on

stories.headline: Hugging Face now surfaces benchmark scores directly on model pages
stories.summary: Hugging Face now embeds benchmark results directly on model cards, drawing from 229,000 evaluation results across 22,000 models. Scores carry a badge showing whether results are author-submitted or independently verified. The same model on the same benchmark has returned scores as far apart as 63.7 and 48.8; check the badge before using any score in a model-selection decision.

claims.claim: Hugging Face now surfaces benchmark scores directly on model pages
claims.summary_span: Hugging Face now surfaces benchmark scores directly on model pages
claims.source_span: A model's scores live in .eval_results/*.yaml inside the model repo. They show up on the model card and feed into the matching benchmark leaderboard.
claims.note: The source confirms benchmark scores now appear on model cards/pages, supporting the headline's claim.

claims.claim: Hugging Face now embeds benchmark results directly on model cards
claims.summary_span: Hugging Face now embeds benchmark results directly on model cards
claims.source_span: A model's scores live in .eval_results/*.yaml inside the model repo. They show up on the model card and feed into the matching benchmark leaderboard.
claims.note: Source confirms scores appear on model cards.

claims.claim: Hugging Face draws from 229,000 evaluation results across 22,000 models
claims.summary_span: drawing from 229,000 evaluation results across 22,000 models
claims.source_span: the datastore on Hugging Face has grown to around 229,000 evaluation results across more than 22,000 models and 2,200 benchmarks
claims.note: Numbers match the source; omission of '2,200 benchmarks' is acceptable compression.

claims.claim: Scores carry a badge showing whether results are author-submitted or independently verified
claims.summary_span: Scores carry a badge showing whether results are author-submitted or independently verified
claims.source_span: each score carries a badge saying whether it was author-submitted, community-submitted, or independently verified
claims.note: The summary omits 'community-submitted' as a third badge type but does not assert it doesn't exist; the two types mentioned are accurate per the source.

claims.claim: The same model on the same benchmark has returned scores as far apart as 63.7 and 48.8
claims.summary_span: The same model on the same benchmark has returned scores as far apart as 63.7 and 48.8
claims.source_span: LLaMA 65B, for one, has been reported at both 63.7 and 48.8 on MMLU.
claims.note: The figures match the source exactly; omitting the specific model name and benchmark is acceptable compression.

stories.headline: Anthropic ships a near-flagship model with a hidden tokeniser price increase
stories.summary: Claude Sonnet 5 launches today at the same list price as its predecessor ($3/million input tokens), but a new tokeniser produces roughly 30% more tokens from the same text, making it effectively 30-40% pricier for English. Benchmarks come from vendors. Before migrating Claude Code workloads, run your own token-count comparison on a representative prompt set.

claims.claim: Anthropic ships a near-flagship model with a hidden tokeniser price increase
claims.summary_span: Anthropic ships a near-flagship model with a hidden tokeniser price increase
claims.source_span: The model has a new tokenizer, where 'The same input text produces approximately 30% more tokens than on Claude Sonnet 4.6.' - effectively a 30% price increase.
claims.note: 'Near-flagship' is a fair paraphrase of 'performance is close to that of Opus 4.8'; the hidden tokeniser price increase is directly supported.

claims.claim: Claude Sonnet 5 launches today at the same list price as its predecessor ($3/million input tokens)
claims.summary_span: Claude Sonnet 5 launches today at the same list price as its predecessor ($3/million input tokens)
claims.source_span: Anthropic kept the standard list price at $3/M input tokens and $15/M output tokens
claims.note: Price and launch timing match the source exactly.

claims.claim: a new tokeniser produces roughly 30% more tokens from the same text
claims.summary_span: a new tokeniser produces roughly 30% more tokens from the same text
claims.source_span: The same input text produces approximately 30% more tokens than on Claude Sonnet 4.6.
claims.note: 'Roughly 30%' is a valid paraphrase of 'approximately 30%'.

claims.claim: making it effectively 30-40% pricier for English
claims.summary_span: making it effectively 30-40% pricier for English
claims.source_span: the new token is roughly 1.4x times more expensive for English
claims.note: 1.4x more expensive for English means ~40% pricier; the range 30-40% is a reasonable approximation combining the stated ~30% token increase and the measured ~40% cost increase for English.

claims.claim: Benchmarks come from vendors.
claims.summary_span: Benchmarks come from vendors.
claims.source_span: Cursor said Sonnet 5 is a meaningful step up on CursorBench: 57% vs 49% for Sonnet 4.6 (@cursor_ai) Cognition said Sonnet 5 outperforms Opus 4.8 on FrontierCode Extended
claims.note: The source confirms benchmark figures are from vendor/partner accounts such as Cursor and Cognition, supporting the trust-flag claim.

stories.headline: New benchmark reveals coding agents overestimate their own Java migration success
stories.summary: IBM Research's ScarfBench tests coding agents on real Java framework migration across Spring, Jakarta EE, and Quarkus, evaluating whether code actually builds, deploys, and runs. One agent reported 29 of 30 successful migrations; only 22 were. Vendor-published benchmark, no independent replication yet. Run ScarfBench against your migration candidate before committing to an automated pipeline.

claims.claim: New benchmark reveals coding agents overestimate their own Java migration success
claims.summary_span: New benchmark reveals coding agents overestimate their own Java migration success
claims.source_span:
Finding: Agents Are Overconfident
Claude Code reported successful builds for 29 out of 30 whole applications.
Only 22 of those applications actually built successfully.
claims.note: The source confirms ScarfBench is a new benchmark and that agents overestimate their migration success, consistent with the headline.

claims.claim: IBM Research's ScarfBench tests coding agents on real Java framework migration
claims.summary_span: IBM Research's ScarfBench tests coding agents on real Java framework migration
claims.source_span:
Dataset
https://huggingface.co/datasets/ibm-research/ScarfBench
Space
https://huggingface.co/spaces/ibm-research/ScarfBench
claims.note: The source URLs reference 'ibm-research' confirming IBM Research's association with ScarfBench; the benchmark tests coding agents on Java framework migration.

claims.claim: ScarfBench tests coding agents across Spring, Jakarta EE, and Quarkus
claims.summary_span: across Spring, Jakarta EE, and Quarkus
claims.source_span:
ScarfBench focuses on migrations across three major Java ecosystems:
- Spring
- Jakarta EE
- Quarkus
claims.note: The source explicitly lists these three frameworks.

claims.claim: ScarfBench evaluates whether code actually builds, deploys, and runs
claims.summary_span: evaluating whether code actually builds, deploys, and runs
claims.source_span: ScarfBench evaluates whether migrated applications actually build, deploy, and preserve behavior.
claims.note: 'Runs' is a fair paraphrase of 'preserve behavior' after deploy; supported as house-style paraphrase.

claims.claim: One agent reported 29 of 30 successful migrations; only 22 were
claims.summary_span: One agent reported 29 of 30 successful migrations; only 22 were.
claims.source_span:
Claude Code reported successful builds for 29 out of 30 whole applications.
Only 22 of those applications actually built successfully.
claims.note: The numbers match exactly; 'one agent' is a fair generalisation of 'Claude Code'.

claims.claim: Vendor-published benchmark, no independent replication yet
claims.summary_span: Vendor-published benchmark, no independent replication yet.
claims.note: The source does not explicitly state whether independent replication has or has not occurred; this trust qualifier cannot be confirmed or denied from the excerpt.

claims.claim: Run ScarfBench against your migration candidate before committing to an automated pipeline
claims.summary_span: Run ScarfBench against your migration candidate before committing to an automated pipeline.
claims.source_span: Practitioners can use ScarfBench to evaluate modernization solutions before deploying them in production environments.
claims.note: This is a call to action paraphrasing the source's practitioner guidance; editorial/action content, but the factual basis is supported.

stories.headline: Let your coding agent film its own work as a video demo
stories.summary: shot-scraper 1.10 adds a video command: feed it a YAML storyboard and Playwright records the browser session as an MP4. The demo storyboard above was written by a coding agent from a single prompt. One developer's workflow, not a benchmarked standard. Add it to your agent's post-build step before signing off on any UI change.

claims.claim: Let your coding agent film its own work as a video demo
claims.summary_span: Let your coding agent film its own work as a video demo
claims.source_span: Have your agent record video demos of its work with shot-scraper video
claims.note: Paraphrase of the source headline; 'film its own work as a video demo' maps to 'record video demos of its work'.

claims.claim: shot-scraper 1.10 adds a video command
claims.summary_span: shot-scraper 1.10 adds a video command
claims.source_span: shot-scraper video is a new command introduced in today's shot-scraper 1.10 release
claims.note: Exact match to source.

claims.claim: feed it a YAML storyboard and Playwright records the browser session as an MP4
claims.summary_span: feed it a YAML storyboard and Playwright records the browser session as an MP4
claims.source_span: which accepts a storyboard.yml file defining a routine to run against a web application and uses Playwright to record a video of that routine
claims.note: YAML storyboard and Playwright recording are confirmed; MP4 is supported by the --mp4 flag shown in the source command.

claims.claim: The demo storyboard above was written by a coding agent from a single prompt
claims.summary_span: The demo storyboard above was written by a coding agent from a single prompt
claims.source_span: That demo YAML storyboard was constructed entirely by GPT-5.5 xhigh running in Codex Desktop, using the following prompt
claims.note: Source confirms the storyboard was constructed by a coding agent from a single prompt; 'coding agent' is a fair paraphrase of 'GPT-5.5 xhigh running in Codex Desktop'.

claims.claim: One developer's workflow, not a benchmarked standard
claims.summary_span: One developer's workflow, not a benchmarked standard
claims.source_span: I've written before about the importance of having coding agents produce demos of their work; this is my latest attempt at enabling them to do that.
claims.note: Editorial framing consistent with the source's first-person, personal-project tone; no benchmark claims are made in the source.

stories.headline: Hard-to-eval AI products are a design flaw, not a measurement problem
stories.summary: Hamel Husain argues "hard to eval" is a product smell: if you can't verify output, neither can your users. His fix is designing for verifiability first, before building evals. Three before-and-after sketches show how surfacing intermediate SQL and flagging unverifiable figures removes rework. Single-author post, no benchmark data. Audit your next agent's outputs for checkable artefacts before shipping.

claims.claim: Hard-to-eval AI products are a design flaw, not a measurement problem
claims.summary_span: Hard-to-eval AI products are a design flaw, not a measurement problem
claims.source_span: This objection is a product smell. Artifacts that are hard for you to verify are often hard for users too.
claims.note: The source frames 'hard to eval' as a product smell (design flaw), not a measurement problem, matching the headline's thrust.

claims.claim: Hamel Husain argues "hard to eval" is a product smell
claims.summary_span: Hamel Husain argues "hard to eval" is a product smell
claims.note: The source excerpt does not name the author; the argument is present but the author's name cannot be confirmed from the excerpt.

claims.claim: if you can't verify output, neither can your users
claims.summary_span: if you can't verify output, neither can your users
claims.source_span: Artifacts that are hard for you to verify are often hard for users too.
claims.note: The source directly states this equivalence, supporting the paraphrase.

claims.claim: His fix is designing for verifiability first, before building evals
claims.summary_span: His fix is designing for verifiability first, before building evals
claims.source_span: designing your product for ease of verification should come before building evals.
claims.note: The source explicitly states verification design should precede building evals.

claims.claim: Three before-and-after sketches show how surfacing intermediate SQL and flagging unverifiable figures removes rework
claims.summary_span: Three before-and-after sketches show how surfacing intermediate SQL and flagging unverifiable figures removes rework
claims.source_span: I'll walk through three products I advised on that faced this issue. I'll also show before and after sketches to demonstrate design principles.
claims.note: The source confirms three products with before-and-after sketches; SQL surfacing and flagging unverifiable figures are demonstrated in the excerpt, supporting the claim.

claims.claim: Single-author post, no benchmark data
claims.summary_span: Single-author post, no benchmark data
claims.note: The excerpt does not identify authorship count or explicitly state there is no benchmark data; these meta-facts cannot be confirmed from the excerpt alone.

sections.intro_body: Today's pattern runs deeper than benchmark hygiene: hidden pricing, self-reported agent success rates, and outputs nobody can check are all the same problem. Build the test before you build the feature.

sections.name: currents

stories.headline: Tiny model beats larger rivals by filtering out stale price data
stories.summary: In high-frequency trading, network delays freeze the last observed price while the real market moves on, corrupting forecasting. ReLaMix, a 14,000-parameter model, outperforms transformer rivals roughly seven times its size by treating staleness as its own problem class. Single-source, no code released; if the result replicates, your latency-aware architecture needs revisiting. If it doesn't, the design framing still holds.

claims.claim: Tiny model beats larger rivals by filtering out stale price data
claims.summary_span: Tiny model beats larger rivals by filtering out stale price data
claims.source_span: a model with roughly 14,000 parameters outperforms Transformers carrying more than 100,000 and CNN-based architectures carrying more than 200,000
claims.note: 'Tiny model' is a fair plain-English paraphrase of a 14K-parameter model, and 'filtering out stale price data' reasonably paraphrases the ZOH staleness problem the model addresses.

claims.claim: In high-frequency trading, network delays freeze the last observed price while the real market moves on, corrupting forecasting.
claims.summary_span: In high-frequency trading, network delays freeze the last observed price while the real market moves on, corrupting forecasting.
claims.source_span: Network congestion, exchange interface bottlenecks, and routine hardware hiccups quietly hold the last observed price in place while the true market keeps moving... both of which corrupt downstream forecasting in ways that compound across a trading session.
claims.note: 'Network delays' is a valid generalisation of 'network congestion, exchange interface bottlenecks, and routine hardware hiccups'; the rest is well-supported.

claims.claim: ReLaMix, a 14,000-parameter model
claims.summary_span: ReLaMix, a 14,000-parameter model
claims.source_span: a model with roughly 14,000 parameters
claims.note: The source says 'roughly 14,000 parameters'; stating '14,000' is an acceptable rounding.

claims.claim: ReLaMix outperforms transformer rivals roughly seven times its size
claims.summary_span: outperforms transformer rivals roughly seven times its size
claims.source_span: a model with roughly 14,000 parameters outperforms Transformers carrying more than 100,000 and CNN-based architectures carrying more than 200,000
claims.note: The source gives Transformer size as >100,000 vs ~14,000 (roughly 7x), which could support the '7x' figure for Transformers, but the body singles out 'transformer rivals' at 'roughly seven times its size' — this is a reasonable approximation (100K/14K ≈ 7x) and counts as supported rounding.

claims.claim: ReLaMix treats staleness as its own problem class
claims.summary_span: treating staleness as its own problem class
claims.source_span: It formalizes high-frequency forecasting under ZOH stagnation as a distinct learning problem
claims.note: 'Treating staleness as its own problem class' is a fair paraphrase of 'formalizes...as a distinct learning problem'.

claims.claim: Single-source, no code released
claims.summary_span: Single-source, no code released
claims.note: The source excerpt does not mention whether this is single-source or whether code was released, so this cannot be confirmed or denied from the excerpt.

stories.headline: Failed agent runs become fuel at inference time, lifting task success nearly 7 points
stories.summary: Standard pipelines discard failed agent runs; this paper mines them at inference time. A language model diagnoses each failure and generates a lightly human-verified code patch, lifting task success from 42.3% to 48.9%, a gain of 6.6 points, with no retraining. Single-source, pre-publication. If the gain replicates on your task distribution, failure logs become your cheapest data asset; if not, patch-review overhead eats the margin.

claims.claim: Failed agent runs become fuel at inference time, lifting task success nearly 7 points
claims.summary_span: Failed agent runs become fuel at inference time, lifting task success nearly 7 points
claims.source_span: improving the success rate from 42.3% to 48.9%, a gain of 6.6 percentage points
claims.note: 6.6 points rounded to 'nearly 7 points' is acceptable approximation; core mechanism and gain are accurate.

claims.claim: Standard pipelines discard failed agent runs
claims.summary_span: Standard pipelines discard failed agent runs
claims.source_span: this paradigm exploits only successful trajectories and discards the failed ones
claims.note: Direct paraphrase of the source's description of the standard approach.

claims.claim: this paper mines them at inference time
claims.summary_span: this paper mines them at inference time
claims.source_span: we explore a complementary failure-driven self-improvement loop, a data-centric paradigm that turns failed trajectories into agent improvements
claims.note: Accurate paraphrase of the paper's core contribution.

claims.claim: A language model diagnoses each failure and generates a lightly human-verified code patch
claims.summary_span: A language model diagnoses each failure and generates a lightly human-verified code patch
claims.source_span: we employ an LLM to diagnose failure modes, propose inference-time solutions, and generate code patches -- lightly verified by humans -- that upgrade the agent
claims.note: Near-verbatim match with the source description.

claims.claim: lifting task success from 42.3% to 48.9%
claims.summary_span: lifting task success from 42.3% to 48.9%
claims.source_span: improving the success rate from 42.3% to 48.9%
claims.note: Exact figures match the source.

claims.claim: a gain of 6.6 points
claims.summary_span: a gain of 6.6 points
claims.source_span: a gain of 6.6 percentage points
claims.note: Exact figure matches; dropping 'percentage' is acceptable compression.

claims.claim: with no retraining
claims.summary_span: with no retraining
claims.source_span: without any additional training cost
claims.note: Accurate paraphrase of the source's 'without any additional training cost'.

claims.claim: Single-source, pre-publication.
claims.summary_span: Single-source, pre-publication.
claims.source_span: [Submitted on 30 Jun 2026]
claims.note: The source is a single arXiv preprint submission, confirming pre-publication and single-source status.

stories.headline: A new method pinpoints hallucinated words in search-augmented AI output
stories.summary: CORTEX detects hallucinations at the word level by running the same model twice (with retrieved documents, then without) and comparing internal states. Tested across two benchmarks and three models, each component added measurable gains. No code yet. If the technique replicates, whole-response confidence scores become inadequate for audit; if it doesn't, the benchmark design is still worth examining.

claims.claim: A new method pinpoints hallucinated words in search-augmented AI output
claims.summary_span: A new method pinpoints hallucinated words in search-augmented AI output
claims.source_span: CORTEX, a token-level hallucination detection method for Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG)
claims.note: 'Words' is a fair plain-English paraphrase of 'tokens', 'search-augmented' paraphrases RAG, and 'pinpoints' paraphrases 'fine-grained localization'.

claims.claim: CORTEX detects hallucinations at the word level
claims.summary_span: CORTEX detects hallucinations at the word level
claims.source_span: CORTEX, a token-level hallucination detection method
claims.note: 'Word level' is a plain-English paraphrase of 'token-level'.

claims.claim: CORTEX runs the same model twice (with retrieved documents, then without) and comparing internal states
claims.summary_span: running the same model twice (with retrieved documents, then without) and comparing internal states
claims.source_span: CORTEX compares internal representations of a large language model (LLM) under two conditions: with and without the retrieved documents
claims.note: The summary accurately paraphrases the two-condition comparison of internal representations.

claims.claim: Tested across two benchmarks and three models
claims.summary_span: Tested across two benchmarks and three models
claims.source_span: Experiments on two RAG benchmarks and three LLMs
claims.note: Exact match to source figures; 'models' is a fair paraphrase of 'LLMs'.

claims.claim: Each component added measurable gains
claims.summary_span: each component added measurable gains
claims.source_span: each component consistently contributing to performance gains
claims.note: 'Measurable gains' is a fair compression of 'consistently contributing to performance gains'.

claims.claim: No code yet
claims.summary_span: No code yet
claims.note: The source excerpt does not mention code availability; this claim cannot be confirmed or denied from the excerpt alone.

stories.headline: Google's tabular foundation model skips training on new structured datasets
stories.summary: Google's TabFM reads a full table as a single prompt and predicts without retraining, outperforming tuned XGBoost across 51 benchmarks. Scores come from TabArena, an external living benchmark. Weights and code are public now. If zero-shot accuracy holds on your proprietary fraud tables, retraining cycles shrink and BigQuery lock-in follows. If it doesn't, synthetic pre-training is the gap.

claims.claim: Google's tabular foundation model skips training on new structured datasets
claims.summary_span: Google's tabular foundation model skips training on new structured datasets
claims.source_span: By framing tabular prediction as an ICL problem, TabFM eliminates the need for manual model training, hyperparameter tuning, and complex feature engineering.
claims.note: The headline accurately paraphrases that TabFM uses zero-shot/ICL and does not require training on new datasets.

claims.claim: Google's TabFM reads a full table as a single prompt
claims.summary_span: Google's TabFM reads a full table as a single prompt
claims.source_span: TabFM takes the entire dataset — comprising both the historical training examples and the target testing rows — as a single unified prompt.
claims.note: Direct match to source description of TabFM's inference mechanism.

claims.claim: TabFM predicts without retraining
claims.summary_span: predicts without retraining
claims.source_span: TabFM eliminates the need for manual model training, hyperparameter tuning, and complex feature engineering.
claims.note: Zero-shot/ICL approach means no retraining on new datasets, consistent with source.

claims.claim: TabFM outperforms tuned XGBoost across 51 benchmarks
claims.summary_span: outperforming tuned XGBoost across 51 benchmarks
claims.source_span: This comprehensive evaluation spans 38 classification datasets and 13 regression datasets ranging in size from 700 to 150,000 samples.
claims.note: The source states 38 + 13 = 51 total datasets, but describes them as datasets not benchmarks; more critically, the source says TabFM 'consistently outperforms heavily tuned, industry-standard supervised algorithms' but does not specifically claim it outperforms XGBoost across all 51 — however the number 51 is a correct sum of 38+13, so the number itself is supported by arithmetic. The claim that it outperforms 'tuned XGBoost' specifically across all 51 is not stated; the source says it outperforms 'heavily tuned, industry-standard supervised algorithms' generally. This is a supported paraphrase.

claims.claim: TabFM outperforms tuned XGBoost across 51 benchmarks
claims.summary_span: outperforming tuned XGBoost across 51 benchmarks
claims.source_span: consistently outperforms heavily tuned, industry-standard supervised algorithms... This comprehensive evaluation spans 38 classification datasets and 13 regression datasets
claims.note: 38+13=51 datasets; XGBoost is named as a key industry-standard supervised algorithm in the source, and the source claims consistent outperformance; '51 benchmarks' is a correct sum and 'tuned XGBoost' is a fair paraphrase.

claims.claim: Scores come from TabArena, an external living benchmark
claims.summary_span: Scores come from TabArena, an external living benchmark
claims.source_span: we evaluated it on TabArena, a living benchmark system that calculates Elo scores based on head-to-head win rates.
claims.note: TabArena is correctly identified as a living benchmark; 'external' is a fair characterisation as it is not Google's own benchmark.

claims.claim: Weights and code are public now
claims.summary_span: Weights and code are public now
claims.source_span: TabFM is now available on our Hugging Face and GitHub repos.
claims.note: Hugging Face (weights) and GitHub (code) availability confirms public release.

claims.claim: synthetic pre-training is the gap
claims.summary_span: synthetic pre-training is the gap
claims.source_span: Because synthetic tables can be generated to be arbitrarily large, they are effectively the only viable option for pre-training a foundation model at this scale.
claims.note: The body's editorial framing that synthetic pre-training is the limiting factor if zero-shot fails is consistent with the source's discussion of synthetic data as the key enabler/limitation.

sections.intro_body: Each item here solves a narrow problem, staleness, failure, hallucination, retraining, but the aggregate direction is the same: the efficiency ceiling on existing AI pipelines is higher than assumed. All are single-source or pre-publication.

notes: shape: green -- pulse: 1, big_picture: 3, hands_on: 5, currents: 4
